name: API lifecycle & security
description: API versioning and backward compatibility, OAuth2/OIDC/JWT, RBAC vs ABAC — from the Dennemeyer system design 100-topic doc.

topics.name: API versioning & compatibility
topics.description: Evolving APIs without breaking consumers: additive changes, versioning strategies, deprecation.

topics.name: OAuth2 / OIDC / JWT
topics.description: Delegated authorization, identity on top of it, and the token format tying them together.

topics.name: RBAC vs ABAC
topics.description: Role-based vs attribute-based access control models and when each fits.

questions.title: Evolving an API without breaking clients
questions.prompt: Your public API needs a breaking change, but hundreds of clients depend on it. How do you handle API evolution?
questions.answerStructureHint: Prefer additive/backward-compatible changes; when truly breaking: version, run both, deprecation policy with communication and migration window.

expectedPoints.label: Prefers backward-compatible changes
expectedPoints.description: Add fields/endpoints instead of changing or removing; tolerant readers ignore unknown fields.

expectedPoints.label: Versions and runs both
expectedPoints.description: Introduces v2 (URL/header), keeps v1 running in parallel during migration.

expectedPoints.label: Deprecation policy with communication
expectedPoints.description: Announce, document, set a sunset date, monitor v1 usage before turning it off.

followUps.prompt: How does the same problem look for event schemas on a message queue?

questions.title: OAuth2, OIDC and JWT
questions.prompt: Explain the relationship between OAuth2, OpenID Connect and JWT. What does each one actually do?
questions.answerStructureHint: OAuth2 = delegated authorization (access tokens), OIDC = identity layer on top (ID token), JWT = self-contained signed token format.

expectedPoints.label: OAuth2 = delegated authorization
expectedPoints.description: Lets an app access resources on a user's behalf via access tokens — authorization, not login.

expectedPoints.label: OIDC adds identity
expectedPoints.description: OpenID Connect layers login/identity on OAuth2 via the ID token and userinfo.

expectedPoints.label: JWT = signed, self-contained token
expectedPoints.description: Signed claims readable without a DB lookup; validate signature and expiry; revocation is the hard part.

followUps.prompt: Where would you store tokens in a browser app, and what are the risks of each option?

questions.title: RBAC vs ABAC
questions.prompt: Compare role-based and attribute-based access control. When does RBAC stop being enough?
questions.answerStructureHint: RBAC: permissions via roles — simple, auditable. ABAC: policies over attributes (user, resource, context) — fine-grained. Role explosion is the tipping point.

expectedPoints.label: RBAC basics and strengths
expectedPoints.description: Users get roles, roles carry permissions; simple to reason about and audit.

expectedPoints.label: ABAC: policies over attributes
expectedPoints.description: Decisions from attributes of user, resource and context (owner, department, time, tenant).

expectedPoints.label: Knows the tipping point
expectedPoints.description: Role explosion — combinatorial roles per department/region/tenant — signals the need for ABAC; ABAC costs complexity and testability.

followUps.prompt: How does the principle of least privilege apply to whichever model you pick?
